question: State these facts about the James Webb Space Telescope, and explicitly flag any you are not fully sure of: (a) its launch date, (b) the location/orbit it operates from, (c) its primary mirror diameter, and (d) the name of its primary near-infrared camera.
category: multi-claim factuality + calibration (several discrete verifiable claims)

round1_answers.name: Claude Haiku 4.5
round1_answers.answer:
I'm confident about all of these facts based on my training knowledge:

**(a) Launch date:** December 25, 2021 (Christmas Day)

**(b) Location/orbit:** It operates from the second Lagrange point (L2), approximately 1 million miles from Earth on the side opposite the Sun. This is a gravitationally stable point where the telescope can maintain a stable position relative to Earth and Sun.

**(c) Primary mirror diameter:** 6.5 meters (approximately 21.3 feet). The mirror is made up of 18 hexagonal beryllium segments coated with gold.

**(d) Primary near-infrared camera:** The Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) is its primary near-infrared imaging instrument.

**Confidence flag:** I am fully confident in all four of these facts. They are well-established, fundamental specifications of the JWST that have been consistent since its launch and operation began.

round1_answers.name: Grok 4.3
round1_answers.answer:
**All facts below are confirmed from official NASA sources and Wikipedia (cross-checked for consistency as of 2026).**

(a) **Launch date**: December 25, 2021 (on an Ariane 5 rocket from Kourou, French Guiana).[[1]](https://science.nasa.gov/mission/webb/)

(b) **Location/orbit**: It operates in a halo orbit around the Sun-Earth L2 Lagrange point, approximately 1.5 million km (about 1 million miles) from Earth—not in Earth orbit like Hubble.[[2]](https://science.nasa.gov/mission/webb/orbit/)

(c) **Primary mirror diameter**: 6.5 meters (composed of 18 hexagonal beryllium segments).[[3]](https://science.nasa.gov/asset/webb/webb-telescopes-mirror-size-and-material/)

(d) **Primary near-infrared camera**: NIRCam (Near Infrared Camera), Webb’s main near-IR imager (0.6–5 μm range).[[4]](https://science.nasa.gov/mission/webb/nircam/)

No uncertainties to flag.

round1_answers.name: GPT-5.4 mini
round1_answers.answer:
Here are the facts about the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), along with notes on the certainty of each:

a) Launch date  
• JWST was launched on **25 December 2021** at 12:20 UTC from the Guiana Space Centre in French Guiana aboard an Ariane 5 rocket. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope?utm_source=openai))  
• Certainty: High. (Multiple reliable sources agree.)

b) The location/orbit it operates from  
• It operates in a halo (or Lissajous-type) orbit around the **Sun–Earth L₂ Lagrange point**, approximately 1.5 million kilometers from Earth, on the side opposite the Sun. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope?utm_source=openai))  
• Certainty: High.

c) Its primary mirror diameter  
• The primary mirror is made up of 18 hexagonal segments that combine to form a mirror approximately **6.5 meters** in diameter (flat-to-flat). ([jwst-docs.stsci.edu](https://jwst-docs.stsci.edu/jwst-observatory-hardware/jwst-telescope?utm_source=openai))  
• Certainty: High.

d) Name of its primary near-infrared camera  
• The primary near-infrared camera is called **NIRCam** (Near-InfraRed Camera). It covers wavelengths from about **0.6 to 5.0 microns**. ([science.nasa.gov](https://science.nasa.gov/mission/webb/nircam?utm_source=openai))  
• Certainty: High.

If you like, I can also provide more details (e.g. resolution, field of view) for these components.

round1_answers.name: Gemini 3.5 Flash
round1_answers.answer:
The goal is to provide four precise, verified physical and operational parameters of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) while explicitly identifying any areas of uncertainty. 

The primary facts are:
*   **(a) Launch Date:** December 25, 2021.
*   **(b) Location/Orbit:** A halo orbit around the Sun-Earth Lagrange Point 2 (L2), which is approximately 1.5 million kilometers (1 million miles) from Earth.
*   **(c) Primary Mirror Diameter:** 6.5 meters (21.3 feet).
*   **(d) Primary Near-Infrared Camera:** NIRCam (Near-Infrared Camera).
*   *Certainty Flag:* I am **100% confident** in all four of these facts; there is no ambiguity or scientific dispute regarding them.

To challenge the assumptions often baked into these metrics, several technical nuances and edge cases must be noted:
1.  **Launch Date:** While universally recorded as December 25, 2021, based on UTC (12:20 UTC) and local Kourou time (9:20 a.m. local), certain eastern time zones experienced the launch on December 26.
2.  **Orbit:** L2 is not a fixed point where the telescope "sits." JWST actually orbits the Sun while continuously looping around the mathematical L2 point in a wide halo orbit to avoid Earth's shadow, ensuring its solar panels remain illuminated.
3.  **Mirror Diameter:** The "6.5-meter" specification is a calculated equivalent diameter representing the total light-collecting area of the 18 hexagonal segments combined. The actual physical edge-to-edge span varies depending on whether you measure flat-to-flat or corner-to-corner across the hexagonal geometry. 
4.  **Primary Camera:** While NIRCam is the primary optical imager and was critical for aligning the telescope's mirror segments, it is not the only near-infrared instrument. It operates alongside NIRSpec (spectrograph) and NIRISS (imager/spectrograph), which share the near-infrared workload.

synthesis.answer:
## James Webb Space Telescope: Key Facts

**(a) Launch Date:** The James Webb Space Telescope launched on **December 25, 2021, at 12:20 UTC** [1][9][17]. It lifted off aboard an Ariane 5 rocket [2][11] from the Guiana Space Centre in Kourou, French Guiana [3][10].

**(b) Location/Orbit:** JWST operates in a **halo orbit around the Sun-Earth L2 (second Lagrange) point** [4][18], situated approximately **1.5 million kilometers (about 1 million miles) from Earth** on the side opposite the Sun [5][13][27]. This gravitationally stable location allows the telescope to maintain a fixed orientation relative to Earth and Sun, which is ideal for its thermal and observational requirements.

**(c) Primary Mirror Diameter:** JWST's primary mirror has a diameter of **6.5 meters (approximately 21.3 feet)** [6][20]. It is composed of **18 hexagonal segments** made of beryllium coated with gold [7][21][31].

**(d) Primary Near-Infrared Camera:** The primary near-infrared camera is called **NIRCam (Near-InfraRed Camera)** [8][16][22][32]. It serves as Webb's primary optical imager and covers wavelengths from approximately **0.6 to 5.0 microns** [8][16].

> ✅ **Confidence note:** All four facts above are well-supported by multiple concordant verified sources and carry high confidence. No claims required flagging as uncertain.

The Sun-Earth L2 Lagrange point is approximately 1.5 million km (about 1 million miles) from Earth.
